name: Vivasvan
description: Vivasvan, also known as Surya, is a prominent solar deity in Hindu mythology, often regarded as the father of humanity and the progenitor of several important dynasties. He is the radiant sun god, embodying light, energy, and life-giving power. Vivasvan's origins trace back to the Adityas, the sons of Aditi, representing celestial light and cosmic order. He is depicted as a golden-hued figure riding a chariot drawn by seven horses, symbolizing the seven colors of light or the seven days of the week. Vivasvan's primary power lies in his ability to illuminate the world, dispel darkness, and sustain life through the energy of the sun. He is considered the source of all warmth and vitality, essential for the growth of plants, the ripening of fruits, and the overall well-being of all living beings. He is also associated with knowledge, wisdom, and cosmic law (dharma). He is revered as a powerful and benevolent deity, responsible for maintaining the balance of the universe and ensuring the continuation of life on Earth. His lineage is significant, as he is the father of Manu (the progenitor of humanity), Yama (the god of death), and the Ashvins (divine healers). Vivasvan is a central figure in Hindu cosmology and is worshipped for his life-sustaining energy and his role in the cyclical nature of time.
subtitle: The Radiant Sun God and Progenitor

keyMyths.title: Birth of Manu
keyMyths.description: Vivasvan is considered the father of Manu, the progenitor of humanity, signifying his role in the creation and continuation of the human race. This establishes his importance in the lineage of mankind.

keyMyths.title: The Sun's Chariot
keyMyths.description: Vivasvan travels across the sky in a chariot drawn by seven horses, representing the seven colors of light and the days of the week. This journey symbolizes the daily cycle of the sun and its continuous provision of light and energy.

keyMyths.title: Vivasvan and Yama
keyMyths.description: Vivasvan is also the father of Yama, the god of death, highlighting the duality of his nature as both a life-giver and a witness to mortality. This connection showcases the cyclical nature of existence.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Hindu mythology and various deities.
sources.text: Provides a comprehensive overview of Hindu mythology and various deities.
